title: Quiz: Weak Measurements
description: Test your understanding of weak measurements, weak values, and their applications

questions.question: What distinguishes a weak measurement from a projective measurement?

options.text: Weak measurements are performed more quickly
Weak measurements use smaller coupling strength, extracting partial information
Weak measurements only work on mixed states
Weak measurements require entanglement with an ancilla

questions.explanation: Weak measurements are characterized by a small coupling between system and meter. This extracts partial information while causing less state disturbance than projective measurements.

questions.question: What is a weak value?

options.text: The expectation value of a weak measurement
The smallest eigenvalue of an observable
The ratio <psi_f|A|psi_i>/<psi_f|psi_i> for pre/post-selected states
The measurement outcome with lowest probability

questions.explanation: The weak value is A_w = <psi_f|A|psi_i>/<psi_f|psi_i>, where psi_i is the pre-selected state and psi_f is the post-selected state. It represents the average meter shift conditioned on successful post-selection.

questions.question: What is remarkable about weak values compared to eigenvalues?

options.text: Weak values are always smaller than eigenvalues
Weak values can be complex and lie outside the eigenvalue range
Weak values are always real numbers
Weak values equal expectation values

questions.explanation: Weak values can be complex and can have real parts that exceed the maximum or minimum eigenvalue of the observable. This is the basis for weak value amplification.

questions.question: What causes weak value amplification?

options.text: Using very sensitive detectors
Post-selecting on states nearly orthogonal to the pre-selected state
Increasing the measurement strength
Using entangled states

questions.explanation: When <psi_f|psi_i> approaches zero (nearly orthogonal states), the weak value A_w = <psi_f|A|psi_i>/<psi_f|psi_i> can become very large, amplifying the meter shift.

questions.question: Weak value amplification provides 'free' signal enhancement without any trade-off.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Large weak values come with low post-selection probability. The signal is amplified, but fewer events pass post-selection. The total information is bounded by quantum limits - amplification redistributes signal, it doesn't create it.

questions.question: For a qubit with Z eigenvalues +1 and -1, what is the maximum possible |Re(Z_w)| with optimal pre/post-selection?

options.text: Exactly 1
At most 2
Unbounded (can be arbitrarily large)
At most sqrt(2)

questions.explanation: The weak value can be arbitrarily large! As the pre- and post-selected states become more orthogonal, the denominator <psi_f|psi_i> approaches zero while the numerator remains finite, making Z_w unbounded.

questions.question: What is continuous weak measurement used for?

options.text: Making projective measurements faster
Monitoring quantum systems in real-time with quantum trajectories
Increasing the dimension of the Hilbert space
Generating entanglement between distant parties

questions.explanation: Continuous weak measurement allows real-time monitoring of quantum systems. Each weak measurement slightly disturbs the state, leading to stochastic quantum trajectories that eventually collapse the state.

questions.question: Which are valid applications of weak measurements? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Measuring tiny spin Hall effect shifts via weak value amplification
Quantum state tomography with reduced disturbance
Testing Leggett-Garg inequalities for temporal correlations
Increasing entanglement between two qubits

questions.explanation: (a) Correct - weak value amplification enables angstrom-scale measurements. (b) Correct - weak tomography preserves coherence better. (c) Correct - weak measurements can test temporal macrorealism. (d) Incorrect - measurements generally don't increase entanglement.
